lens.included_activities: Independent US lower-middle-market manufacturers that repeatedly supply external business customers with rubber tubing, industrial roll coverings, sheeting, roofing membrane, reclaimed rubber, or other specification-based rubber products included in 326299.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive plants, non-control vehicles, one-off or commodity-only operations without repeat customer relationships, and consumer or medical-product businesses such as balloons, floor mats, hair products, pacifiers, and contraceptive devices whose regulation, channels, and demand economics differ materially.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat purchase orders, distributor supply, approved-supplier relationships, and recurring industrial programs for physical rubber products, priced per unit, length, area, weight, lot, or customer specification.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines industrial inputs, building products, reclaimed material, consumer goods, and regulated personal or medical products. It is narrowed to recurring B2B outsourced industrial-product manufacturers so customer economics and operator requirements are coherent rather than blended across incompatible models.

a.rationale: Broader plastics-and-rubber employment is concentrated in machine operation, assembly, inspection, packaging, and production supervision. For the narrowed industrial lens, AI exposure is mainly quoting, specification review, scheduling, purchasing, quality analysis, inspection assistance, maintenance triage, documentation, and administration rather than rubber mixing, forming, cutting, handling, and finishing.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from 10-15 eligible 326299 plants, with current automation status and task-level time data, would materially narrow the range.
caveats: BLS occupational evidence is for NAICS 326, not 326299 or the narrowed lens.
The highly varied production processes could produce materially different exposure by product segment.

rho.rationale: Assisted quoting, order entry, scheduling, document review, inspection triage, and knowledge retrieval are plausible within five years. Implementation is constrained by legacy plant systems, sparse defect data, process variability, customer qualification, environmental and safety requirements, and continued human exception handling.
rho.change_evidence: Sustained pre/post labor-hour results from AI deployments in industrial rubber plants across quoting, planning, quality, maintenance, and administration would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No eligible-lens implementation cohort was found.
Capital-intensive physical automation is excluded unless AI is the enabling constraint.

e.rationale: The official definition includes many business models outside the coherent recurring B2B industrial lens, so fewer than half of size-band firms are assumed eligible. Further exclusions arise from captive operations, weak recurrence, concentrated or owner-dependent customer relationships, and operations lacking transferable compliance or technical systems.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm classification of the supplied 203 candidates by product, channel, independence, repeat B2B revenue, certifications, customer concentration, and owner dependence would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: The supplied firm count is an ESTIMATE and the establishment-based NAICS definition does not reveal firm revenue mix.
Product heterogeneity makes classification error especially likely.

s5.rationale: A meaningful minority of eligible private industrial-rubber firms may change control in five years, while family succession, long owner tenures, strategic ownership, environmental diligence, and unattractive customer or product concentration suppress transaction probability. Minority recaps and internal restructurings are excluded.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-matched five-year control-transfer census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, broker records, and verified ownership histories for eligible firms would materially update the probability.
caveats: No directly observed transfer rate for eligible 326299 firms was found.
Small private deals and failed sale processes are underreported.

q.rationale: Unit, weight, and specification-based pricing can preserve internal cost savings, especially for qualified or custom industrial products. Standardized tubing, sheeting, reclaimed material, and distributor-sold products face rebids, index-linked input pricing, customer procurement pressure, and import competition that pass benefits through over time.
q.change_evidence: Contract and invoice analysis by product segment covering price-reset cadence, resin or rubber indexation, cost-down terms, open-book clauses, and margin persistence after productivity projects would materially change the range.
caveats: The official definition establishes product scope, not actual contract terms.
Capture likely varies more across this residual code than within more homogeneous manufacturing industries.

d5.rationale: The narrowed basket includes recurring industrial, construction, and replacement inputs, supporting broadly stable real demand. End-market cyclicality, imports, building cycles, material substitution, customer redesign, and reclaimed-material economics create downside. BLS reports broader NAICS 326 real-output declines in 2022-2025, including 3.4% in 2025.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price shipment forecasts by eligible product segment, customer end-market, replacement share, construction exposure, and import penetration would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Historical BLS output changes cover all NAICS 326 and are not a five-year forecast.
The narrowed product basket still spans industrial, construction, and recycled-material cycles.

o.rationale: The year-five basket consists of physical rubber products whose compounding, forming, finishing, testing, traceability, and delivery require an accountable producer. Software can alter workflow but cannot replace the supplied material object; a limited share may disappear through self-production, redesign, alternate materials, or channel substitution.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews on make-versus-buy, material substitution, product redesign, and onsite fabrication plans across tubing, roll covering, sheeting, roofing, and reclaimed-rubber end markets would test displacement.
caveats: The residual code contains products with different substitution threats.
Continued operator need does not imply unchanged labor intensity or production methods.

narrative.executive_view: This residual code is too heterogeneous for one coherent commercial screen, so the lens is limited to repeat B2B industrial-product manufacturers. Within that lens, physical-product demand supports operator durability, but AI addresses mainly information workflows around a production-heavy labor base.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist quotes, specifications, scheduling, procurement, defect review, corrective actions, maintenance troubleshooting, order processing, and plant knowledge. Human work remains essential in compounding, setup, forming, cutting, handling, testing, and production exceptions.
narrative.value_capture: Custom and qualification-sensitive products can retain savings between negotiations. Commodity tubing, sheeting, reclaimed rubber, and distributor channels face stronger input-indexing, rebid, and price-competition pressure.
narrative.firm_availability: The supplied dataset estimates 203 firms in the EBITDA band, but the diverse NAICS basket means only a minority-to-majority subset fits recurring B2B outsourced industrial supply. Ownership and deal evidence are not denominator matched.
narrative.demand_durability: Industrial, construction, and replacement uses sustain physical demand, while cycles, imports, redesign, and substitute materials can reduce particular segments. Software is not a substitute for the finished rubber object or accountable producer.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main risk is aggregation across unlike products and channels. Six-digit occupation mix, eligible-firm count, AI results, contract pass-through, environmental liabilities, and control transfers all need direct diligence.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring demand for physical industrial rubber inputs preserves the need for an accountable supplier across the five-year horizon.
narrative.principal_weakness: The residual NAICS category is highly heterogeneous, making eligibility and commercial-retention assumptions unusually uncertain.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 326299 All Other Rubber Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: Shows the code's heterogeneous scope, including rubber tubing, latex foam, reclaimed rubber, consumer goods, contraceptive devices, sheeting, roofing membrane, industrial roll coverings, and other rubber products, plus the exclusions to adjacent industries.

sources.title: Industries at a Glance: Plastics and Rubber Products Manufacturing: NAICS 326
sources.supports: Shows 2025 subsector employment concentrated in machine operators, assemblers, inspectors, packers, and production supervisors, and reports real-output changes of -3.6%, -6.3%, -1.8%, and -3.4% for 2022-2025.
